does: This paper analyzes how imperfect reward signals affect policy gradient optimization in language model training. Rather than treating all reward errors as equally harmful, the authors develop a theoretical framework that categorizes errors by their actual impact on ground truth reward improvement. They prove that some deviations from the true reward function can be benign or even beneficial, particularly when they prevent policies from converging to mediocre solutions. The analysis characterizes which outputs receive probability mass during policy gradient updates and how different types of errors influence this distribution. Based on this theory, the authors introduce new evaluation metrics for reward models used in reinforcement learning from human feedback that better predict downstream policy performance than standard ranking accuracy metrics.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature addressing the practical challenge of reward misspecification in language model training via reinforcement learning. It extends recent work on RLHF and proxy reward learning by providing theoretical foundations for understanding when and why imperfect rewards can still guide effective policy optimization. The paper connects to broader research on reward design, inverse reinforcement learning, and the gap between proxy and true objectives. It also relates to theoretical analyses of policy gradient methods and their convergence properties. The categorization framework addresses limitations in existing reward evaluation approaches that treat errors uniformly, building on prior work examining ranking metrics and their relationship to downstream performance in language model alignment.
enables: This theoretical framework enables more principled design of reward models and evaluation procedures for RLHF systems. The ability to distinguish harmful from benign or beneficial errors could improve how researchers select and train reward models, potentially reducing the sample efficiency and computational cost of alignment pipelines. The work opens avenues for developing better proxy rewards that intentionally exploit beneficial error properties rather than attempting perfect accuracy. It also suggests new directions for understanding policy gradient dynamics in high-dimensional settings and how approximate guidance can outperform seemingly more accurate alternatives. The metrics and insights could facilitate safer deployment of RL-trained language models by improving confidence in reward model quality assessments.
